headline: Markets Brace for Sixth Straight Night of U.S.-Iran Strikes as Semiconductor Rout Clouds the Open
body_html:
Equity futures are trading cautiously in early Friday pre-market after Thursday's session delivered a sharply bifurcated tape: two-thirds of the universe's 456 names closed higher, yet the semiconductor sector cratered 5.1%, dragging the most visible losers down by double digits and offsetting broad strength in homebuilders, consumer names, and healthcare. The overnight backdrop has done little to calm nerves — U.S. forces completed a sixth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, and Iran responded with fresh attacks of its own, sustaining the conflict that has already rattled global energy markets and supply chains.

Oil is rising in pre-market trade as the threat of a Strait of Hormuz closure intensifies. The IEA chief warned Friday that global energy security is at risk if the waterway does not reopen within weeks — a statement likely to keep energy volatility elevated at the open even as the sector itself slipped 0.52% on Thursday. Gold, meanwhile, is on track for its steepest weekly decline in six months as inflation anxiety displaces safe-haven buying.

The primary catalysts to watch at Friday's open are any incremental headlines on Hormuz transit, the trajectory of chip-sector sentiment following Thursday's broad semi selloff, and whether Thursday's freight-and-logistics surge — JB Hunt and Hub Group each rallied more than 7.5% — signals a durable rotation into economically sensitive industrials or simply a one-session relief trade.

briefs.headline: Strait of Hormuz Warning Puts Energy Markets on Edge
briefs.body: The head of the International Energy Agency cautioned Friday that global energy security faces serious risk if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for several more weeks. Oil prices moved higher overnight on the warning, compounding pressure from the six-day-old U.S.-Iran conflict. Traders will be watching crude futures closely at the New York open.

briefs.headline: Semiconductors Suffer Worst Sector Day as Chip Names Crater
briefs.body: The semiconductor sector fell 5.1% on Thursday, the steepest decline among all groups tracked in our universe. MaxLinear dropped 16.25%, Alpha and Omega Semiconductor lost 13.13%, and Sandisk slid 12.63%. The breadth of the selloff — spanning memory, power, and equipment subsectors — suggests sector-wide pressure rather than isolated, company-specific events.

briefs.headline: Freight and Logistics Names Stage Broad Rally on Thursday
briefs.body: Intermodal and trucking stocks powered higher in Thursday's session, with JB Hunt surging 8.01% and Hub Group adding 7.55%. XPO Logistics also climbed 7.06%, rounding out a notable sweep across freight subsectors. Analysts will be watching whether the move reflects improving volume expectations or a rotation away from technology into cyclicals.

briefs.headline: Gold Set for Sharpest Weekly Drop in Six Months
briefs.body: Bullion is heading toward its largest weekly loss in roughly six months as Middle East conflict stokes inflation concerns and diverts flows away from traditional safe-haven assets. The counterintuitive selloff reflects market anxiety that prolonged hostilities will prove inflationary rather than deflationary, pressuring real returns on non-yielding assets.

briefs.headline: Iran War Disrupts China's Oil Import Flows
briefs.body: China's petroleum imports have fallen sharply since the onset of the Iran conflict, raising questions about how quickly flows can recover if and when hostilities ease. The disruption adds another layer of complexity to global crude balances already strained by Hormuz transit uncertainty and elevated U.S. military activity in the region.

briefs.headline: Homebuilders and Consumer Shares Lead Broad Thursday Advance
briefs.body: Even as semiconductors faltered, homebuilders gained 2.32% and consumer names advanced 2.16% on Thursday, helping lift overall breadth to 306 advancers versus 149 decliners across the 456-name universe. Healthcare added 1.88% and software rose 1.7%, suggesting that rotation into domestic-demand sectors remained intact heading into Friday's open.
